1: <header>Environmental life cycle comparisons of pig farming integrated with anaerobic digestion and algae-based wastewater treatment </header>
Wei Wu , Liang-Chiung Cheng Jo-Shu Chang a,b

<list>a Department of Chemical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, 70101, Taiwan 
b Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering, Tunghai University, Taichung 407, Taiwan</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Pig farming Manure Algae Nutrient recovery|This article shows environmental life cycle comparisons of four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) for the intensive pig farming systems in terms of materials, transport, energy, and emissions. The MMS scenarios are categorized by different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based wastewater treatment (AWWT), where the conventional MMS scenario merely uses the outside storage (Sce- nario 1) or adopts an integrated anaerobic mono-digester and the outside storage (Scenario 2). The noncon- ventional MMS scenario uses an integrated anaerobic mono-digester and AWWT process (Scenario 3), notably the harvested algae biomass is used to fully substitute a few ingredients of swine diets. Using life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology, it is validated that Scenario 3 reduces the total environmental impact by 35.5%-40% as compared to Scenarios 1 and 2 and increases the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio from 58% (Scenario 1) to 81%. Scenario 4 uses an integrated anaerobic co-digester and AWWT process, where the harvested algae biomass is partially recycled to substitute the partial ingredients of swine diets and the remaining part is fed into the anaerobic co-digester for improving the biogas yield, to increase the algae biomass yield by 49% and also enhance the cogeneration of heat and power by 26.6% and 7%, respectively, as compared to Scenario 3.
2: Intensive livestock production approach has a significant impact on the environment owing to methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20) as major sources of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and excess manure that need to be effectively treated. A case study of pig farming in the EU validated that vigilant farming practices had a high potential to reduce fossil energy consumption and emissions by improving processes of feed production, manure management, and manure utilization (Nguyen et al., 2010). In general, the feed production process was typically responsible for the severe environmental impacts in pig farming systems (McAuliffe et al., 2016). To address lower GHG emissions, a study by life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology showed that a low-protein diet supplemented with amino acid (AA) could reduce total environmental impacts such as GHG emissions and acidification potential (Ogino et al., 2013), the nutrients recycling by composting for land application could result in lower environmental impacts (Luo et al., 2014), and the sub- stitution of synthetic fertilizers does prevent marine eutrophication (Corbala-Roblesa et al., 2018).
In recent years, environmental impacts of intensive pig farming

systems were often addressed. Winkler et al. (2016) indicated that the environmental burden was primarily related to the farming stage due to its high contribution to global warming potential (GWP), soil acidifi- cation, and eutrophication potential (EP). A cradle-to-gate analysis was carried out to evaluate the environmental impacts of the pig fattening farms in Galicia (Northwest Spain). It showed that the fattening farm activities contributed to most of the environmental effects in almost all the impact categories and the concentrated feed production was found as the environmental hotspot (Noya et al., 2017). Through LCA of the pig manure treatment technologies, a study indicated that the main environmental hotspots were in-house storage, outside storage, and wastewater treatment. For reducing the environmental impacts, short- ening the in-house storage time, covering the storage pool, and improving utilization ratio in field applications were recommended (Yuan et al., 2018). Besides, biogas production is an effective option to reduce GHG emissions since it could substitute for natural gas and transport fuels after upgrading biogas to biomethane. Swine wastewater has recently received increased research atten- tion, especially the microalgae-based processes have potential advan- tages for the clean-up of swine wastewater, and the anaerobic digestion process is beneficial for nitrogen removal (Nagarajan et al., 2019). Microalgae have been recognized as one of the most promising third-generation biofuels because of their ability to grow rapidly, adaptability in low-quality water, CO2 mitigation, and cultivation on non-arable land (Rogers et al., 2014). They are also proficient in removing nitrogen, phosphorus, and toxic metals from wastewater under controlled environments (Cai et al., 2013). Besides, microalgal biomass not only provided natural ingredients and supplements for animal feed, it could also replace synthetic additives in the feed, and improve animal growth and meat quality (Madeira et al., 2017). A study showed that weanling pigs can tolerate the incorporation of algal nu- trients, e.g. the diet with a 7.5%-15% replacement of cereal and soybean meal with algal nutrients (Gatrell et al., 2014). Microalgae cultivation by exploiting nutrients from wastewaters often required large amounts

of N and P, in which nutrients can turn biomass into high fertilizers (Barbera et al., 2018). A few studies indicated that the microalgae cultivation process was enhanced by a specific swine manure dilution ratio (Kumar et al., 2018), and the wastewater-grown Chlorella sp. and Scenedesmus sp. were often treated as potential nutrient sources (Lum et al., 2013). Moreover, a study indicated that integration of algae cultivation and anaerobic digestion could improve methane yield, where a nutrient-rich hydrothermal liquefaction process wastewater was treated as a growth media for the microalgae cultivation (Yang et al., 2018).

The integration of functional anaerobic digestion and farms may become a popular way of producing energy and agricultural products. A case study showed that the fish pond with manure-based digestate instead of fresh manure may be a more efficient way to reduce pelleted fish feed consumption (Nhu et al., 2015). Recently, an integrated treatment process design was used to remove the particles and reduce phosphorus in anaerobically digested swine manure in which algae and bacteria provided excellent biological treatment (Wang et al., 2019). Co-digestion of the manure with carbon-rich waste streams could in- crease electricity production and keep the nutrients of production. The

In this article, the process integration of intensive pig farming sys- items, which is composed of feed sources, pig fattening processes, and manure management systems, is presented. Four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) are described as different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based waste- water treatment (AWWT). The scenario analysis of the intensive pig farming systems based on four MMS scenarios in terms of the effects of the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass, the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio, and the environmental impact analysis are addressed. Finally, the environmental life cycle compari- sons of Scenarios 1-4 concerning the manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and feed production are evaluated.

Swine wastewater has recently received increased research atten- tion, especially the microalgae-based processes have potential advan- tages for the clean-up of swine wastewater, and the anaerobic digestion process is beneficial for nitrogen removal (Nagarajan et al., 2019). Microalgae have been recognized as one of the most promising third-generation biofuels because of their ability to grow rapidly, adaptability in low-quality water, CO2 mitigation, and cultivation on non-arable land (Rogers et al., 2014). They are also proficient in removing nitrogen, phosphorus, and toxic metals from wastewater under controlled environments (Cai et al., 2013). Besides, microalgal biomass not only provided natural ingredients and supplements for animal feed, it could also replace synthetic additives in the feed, and improve animal growth and meat quality (Madeira et al., 2017). A study showed that weanling pigs can tolerate the incorporation of algal nu- trients, e.g. the diet with a 7.5%-15% replacement of cereal and soybean meal with algal nutrients (Gatrell et al., 2014). Microalgae cultivation by exploiting nutrients from wastewaters often required large amounts

of N and P, in which nutrients can turn biomass into high fertilizers (Barbera et al., 2018). A few studies indicated that the microalgae cultivation process was enhanced by a specific swine manure dilution ratio (Kumar et al., 2018), and the wastewater-grown Chlorella sp. and Scenedesmus sp. were often treated as potential nutrient sources (Lum et al., 2013). Moreover, a study indicated that integration of algae cultivation and anaerobic digestion could improve methane yield, where a nutrient-rich hydrothermal liquefaction process wastewater was treated as a growth media for the microalgae cultivation (Yang et al., 2018).

The integration of functional anaerobic digestion and farms may become a popular way of producing energy and agricultural products. A case study showed that the fish pond with manure-based digestate instead of fresh manure may be a more efficient way to reduce pelleted fish feed consumption (Nhu et al., 2015). Recently, an integrated treatment process design was used to remove the particles and reduce phosphorus in anaerobically digested swine manure in which algae and bacteria provided excellent biological treatment (Wang et al., 2019). Co-digestion of the manure with carbon-rich waste streams could in- crease electricity production and keep the nutrients of production. The

In this article, the process integration of intensive pig farming sys- items, which is composed of feed sources, pig fattening processes, and manure management systems, is presented. Four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) are described as different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based waste- water treatment (AWWT). The scenario analysis of the intensive pig farming systems based on four MMS scenarios in terms of the effects of the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass, the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio, and the environmental impact analysis are addressed. Finally, the environmental life cycle compari- sons of Scenarios 1-4 concerning the manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and feed production are evaluated.
5: Intensive livestock production approach has a significant impact on the environment owing to methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20) as major sources of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and excess manure that need to be effectively treated. A case study of pig farming in the EU validated that vigilant farming practices had a high potential to reduce fossil energy consumption and emissions by improving processes of feed production, manure management, and manure utilization (Nguyen et al., 2010). In general, the feed production process was typically responsible for the severe environmental impacts in pig farming systems (McAuliffe et al., 2016). To address lower GHG emissions, a study by life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology showed that a low-protein diet supplemented with amino acid (AA) could reduce total environmental impacts such as GHG emissions and acidification potential (Ogino et al., 2013), the nutrients recycling by composting for land application could result in lower environmental impacts (Luo et al., 2014), and the sub- stitution of synthetic fertilizers does prevent marine eutrophication (Corbala-Roblesa et al., 2018).
In recent years, environmental impacts of intensive pig farming

systems were often addressed. Winkler et al. (2016) indicated that the environmental burden was primarily related to the farming stage due to its high contribution to global warming potential (GWP), soil acidifi- cation, and eutrophication potential (EP). A cradle-to-gate analysis was carried out to evaluate the environmental impacts of the pig fattening farms in Galicia (Northwest Spain). It showed that the fattening farm activities contributed to most of the environmental effects in almost all the impact categories and the concentrated feed production was found as the environmental hotspot (Noya et al., 2017). Through LCA of the pig manure treatment technologies, a study indicated that the main environmental hotspots were in-house storage, outside storage, and wastewater treatment. For reducing the environmental impacts, short- ening the in-house storage time, covering the storage pool, and improving utilization ratio in field applications were recommended (Yuan et al., 2018). Besides, biogas production is an effective option to reduce GHG emissions since it could substitute for natural gas and transport fuels after upgrading biogas to biomethane. To address lower GHG emissions, a study by life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology showed that a low-protein diet supplemented with amino acid (AA) could reduce total environmental impacts such as GHG emissions and acidification potential (Ogino et al., 2013), the nutrients recycling by composting for land application could result in lower environmental impacts (Luo et al., 2014), and the sub- stitution of synthetic fertilizers does prevent marine eutrophication (Corbala-Roblesa et al., 2018).
In recent years, environmental impacts of intensive pig farming

systems were often addressed. Winkler et al. (2016) indicated that the environmental burden was primarily related to the farming stage due to its high contribution to global warming potential (GWP), soil acidifi- cation, and eutrophication potential (EP). A cradle-to-gate analysis was carried out to evaluate the environmental impacts of the pig fattening farms in Galicia (Northwest Spain). It showed that the fattening farm activities contributed to most of the environmental effects in almost all the impact categories and the concentrated feed production was found as the environmental hotspot (Noya et al., 2017). Through LCA of the pig manure treatment technologies, a study indicated that the main environmental hotspots were in-house storage, outside storage, and wastewater treatment. For reducing the environmental impacts, short- ening the in-house storage time, covering the storage pool, and improving utilization ratio in field applications were recommended (Yuan et al., 2018). Besides, biogas production is an effective option to reduce GHG emissions since it could substitute for natural gas and transport fuels after upgrading biogas to biomethane. Swine wastewater has recently received increased research atten- tion, especially the microalgae-based processes have potential advan- tages for the clean-up of swine wastewater, and the anaerobic digestion process is beneficial for nitrogen removal (Nagarajan et al., 2019). Microalgae have been recognized as one of the most promising third-generation biofuels because of their ability to grow rapidly, adaptability in low-quality water, CO2 mitigation, and cultivation on non-arable land (Rogers et al., 2014). They are also proficient in removing nitrogen, phosphorus, and toxic metals from wastewater under controlled environments (Cai et al., 2013). Besides, microalgal biomass not only provided natural ingredients and supplements for animal feed, it could also replace synthetic additives in the feed, and improve animal growth and meat quality (Madeira et al., 2017). A study showed that weanling pigs can tolerate the incorporation of algal nu- trients, e.g. the diet with a 7.5%-15% replacement of cereal and soybean meal with algal nutrients (Gatrell et al., 2014). Microalgae cultivation by exploiting nutrients from wastewaters often required large amounts

of N and P, in which nutrients can turn biomass into high fertilizers (Barbera et al., 2018). A few studies indicated that the microalgae cultivation process was enhanced by a specific swine manure dilution ratio (Kumar et al., 2018), and the wastewater-grown Chlorella sp. and Scenedesmus sp. were often treated as potential nutrient sources (Lum et al., 2013). Moreover, a study indicated that integration of algae cultivation and anaerobic digestion could improve methane yield, where a nutrient-rich hydrothermal liquefaction process wastewater was treated as a growth media for the microalgae cultivation (Yang et al., 2018).

The integration of functional anaerobic digestion and farms may become a popular way of producing energy and agricultural products. A case study showed that the fish pond with manure-based digestate instead of fresh manure may be a more efficient way to reduce pelleted fish feed consumption (Nhu et al., 2015). Recently, an integrated treatment process design was used to remove the particles and reduce phosphorus in anaerobically digested swine manure in which algae and bacteria provided excellent biological treatment (Wang et al., 2019). Co-digestion of the manure with carbon-rich waste streams could in- crease electricity production and keep the nutrients of production. The

In this article, the process integration of intensive pig farming sys- items, which is composed of feed sources, pig fattening processes, and manure management systems, is presented. Four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) are described as different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based waste- water treatment (AWWT). The scenario analysis of the intensive pig farming systems based on four MMS scenarios in terms of the effects of the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass, the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio, and the environmental impact analysis are addressed. Finally, the environmental life cycle compari- sons of Scenarios 1-4 concerning the manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and feed production are evaluated.

Microalgae have been recognized as one of the most promising third-generation biofuels because of their ability to grow rapidly, adaptability in low-quality water, CO2 mitigation, and cultivation on non-arable land (Rogers et al., 2014). They are also proficient in removing nitrogen, phosphorus, and toxic metals from wastewater under controlled environments (Cai et al., 2013). Besides, microalgal biomass not only provided natural ingredients and supplements for animal feed, it could also replace synthetic additives in the feed, and improve animal growth and meat quality (Madeira et al., 2017). In this article, the process integration of intensive pig farming sys- items, which is composed of feed sources, pig fattening processes, and manure management systems, is presented. Four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) are described as different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based waste- water treatment (AWWT). The scenario analysis of the intensive pig farming systems based on four MMS scenarios in terms of the effects of the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass, the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio, and the environmental impact analysis are addressed. Finally, the environmental life cycle compari- sons of Scenarios 1-4 concerning the manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and feed production are evaluated.

In this article, the process integration of intensive pig farming sys- items, which is composed of feed sources, pig fattening processes, and manure management systems, is presented. Four scenarios of manure management systems (MMS) are described as different combinations of outside storage, anaerobic mono-/co-digester, and algae-based waste- water treatment (AWWT). The scenario analysis of the intensive pig farming systems based on four MMS scenarios in terms of the effects of the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass, the nutrient recovery of nitrogen ratio, and the environmental impact analysis are addressed. Finally, the environmental life cycle compari- sons of Scenarios 1-4 concerning the manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and feed production are evaluated.
14: Intensive livestock production approach has a significant impact on the environment owing to methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20) as major sources of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and excess manure that need to be effectively treated. A case study of pig farming in the EU validated that vigilant farming practices had a high potential to reduce fossil energy consumption and emissions by improving processes of feed production, manure management, and manure utilization (Nguyen et al., 2010). In general, the feed production process was typically responsible for the severe environmental impacts in pig farming systems (McAuliffe et al., 2016). To address lower GHG emissions, a study by life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology showed that a low-protein diet supplemented with amino acid (AA) could reduce total environmental impacts such as GHG emissions and acidification potential (Ogino et al., 2013), the nutrients recycling by composting for land application could result in lower environmental impacts (Luo et al., 2014), and the sub- stitution of synthetic fertilizers does prevent marine eutrophication (Corbala-Roblesa et al., 2018).
In recent years, environmental impacts of intensive pig farming

systems were often addressed. Winkler et al. (2016) indicated that the environmental burden was primarily related to the farming stage due to its high contribution to global warming potential (GWP), soil acidifi- cation, and eutrophication potential (EP). A cradle-to-gate analysis was carried out to evaluate the environmental impacts of the pig fattening farms in Galicia (Northwest Spain). It showed that the fattening farm activities contributed to most of the environmental effects in almost all the impact categories and the concentrated feed production was found as the environmental hotspot (Noya et al., 2017). Through LCA of the pig manure treatment technologies, a study indicated that the main environmental hotspots were in-house storage, outside storage, and wastewater treatment. For reducing the environmental impacts, short- ening the in-house storage time, covering the storage pool, and improving utilization ratio in field applications were recommended (Yuan et al., 2018). Besides, biogas production is an effective option to reduce GHG emissions since it could substitute for natural gas and transport fuels after upgrading biogas to biomethane. Notably, anaer- obic digestion can convert organic wastes into biogas which has become a popular and cost-effective process (Pérez-Camacho et al., 2019). Currently, Ramirez-Islas et al. (2020) showed that the pig manure

Swine wastewater has recently received increased research atten- tion, especially the microalgae-based processes have potential advan- tages for the clean-up of swine wastewater, and the anaerobic digestion process is beneficial for nitrogen removal (Nagarajan et al., 2019). Microalgae have been recognized as one of the most promising third-generation biofuels because of their ability to grow rapidly, adaptability in low-quality water, CO2 mitigation, and cultivation on non-arable land (Rogers et al., 2014). They are also proficient in removing nitrogen, phosphorus, and toxic metals from wastewater under controlled environments (Cai et al., 2013). Besides, microalgal biomass not only provided natural ingredients and supplements for animal feed, it could also replace synthetic additives in the feed, and improve animal growth and meat quality (Madeira et al., 2017). A study showed that weanling pigs can tolerate the incorporation of algal nu- trients, e.g. the diet with a 7.5%-15% replacement of cereal and soybean meal with algal nutrients (Gatrell et al., 2014). Microalgae cultivation by exploiting nutrients from wastewaters often required large amounts

of N and P, in which nutrients can turn biomass into high fertilizers (Barbera et al., 2018). A few studies indicated that the microalgae cultivation process was enhanced by a specific swine manure dilution ratio (Kumar et al., 2018), and the wastewater-grown Chlorella sp. and Scenedesmus sp. were often treated as potential nutrient sources (Lum et al., 2013). Moreover, a study indicated that integration of algae cultivation and anaerobic digestion could improve methane yield, where a nutrient-rich hydrothermal liquefaction process wastewater was treated as a growth media for the microalgae cultivation (Yang et al., 2018).

The integration of functional anaerobic digestion and farms may become a popular way of producing energy and agricultural products. A case study showed that the fish pond with manure-based digestate instead of fresh manure may be a more efficient way to reduce pelleted fish feed consumption (Nhu et al., 2015). Recently, an integrated treatment process design was used to remove the particles and reduce phosphorus in anaerobically digested swine manure in which algae and bacteria provided excellent biological treatment (Wang et al., 2019). Co-digestion of the manure with carbon-rich waste streams could in- crease electricity production and keep the nutrients of production. The

15: According to the pig farming in the EU as a case study (Nguyen et al., 2010), the intensive pig farming systems are usually composed of feed sources, pig fattening process, and manure management system (MMS). In general, the main ingredients of swine diets include cereals, soybean meal, additives, and a few minerals (Reese et al., 2010). In the pig fattening process, the pig housing unit consumes electricity, heat, and feedstuff to produce pork after slaughtering and generates raw manure

which can be stored in an in-house storage unit. The traditional MMS is used to recover nitrogen and phosphorous compounds from manure for environmental compliance. By Fig. 1, the new MMS shows the process integration of anaerobic mono-/co-digester and algae-based wastewater treatment (AWWT) processes which can improve the biogas yield and provide the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass. Notably, the combined heat and power (CHP) unit is a renewable energy conversion option for generating heat and electricity by consuming biogas. In this study, four MMS scenarios integrated with the intensive pig farming system are denoted as Scenarios 1-4 shown in Fig. 2, are described as follows.

Scenario 1: The effluent of manure slurry from the in-house storage (MHSout) becomes soil fertilizer through the conventional outside stor- age and composting processes. Organic fertilizer can avoid or reduce synthetic fertilizer manufacture. The energy demand of transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and two emis- sions including OSemit as emissions of outside storage and CPemit as emissions of the composting are taken into consideration.

Scenario 2: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic mono-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue (AMDLS,out) The biogas flow (AMDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and elec- tricity through the CHP unit. The residue is a mixed liquid/solid diges- tate that can be used to produce soil fertilizer through the conventional outside storage and composting processes. The energy demand for the transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and two emissions from the outside storage and the composting are taken into consideration.

Scenario 3: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic mono-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue. The biogas flow (AMDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and electricity by the CHP unit. The residue is separated to liquid digestate (AMDL,out) and solid digestate (AMDs,out) via a sedimentation tank (Rathnasiri, 2016). The solid digestate is directly used to produce soil fertilizer by the composting process and liquid digestate is treated as the nutrient source for the growth of algae in the high rate algae pond (HRAP). The algae biomass is harvested through the settler and centrifugation units. The harvested algae is recycled to fully substitute partial nutrients and ad- ditives of swine diets. The energy demand of transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and one emission from the composting is taken into consideration.

Scenario 4: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic co-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue. The biogas flow (ACDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and electricity through the CHP unit. The residue is separated to liquid digestate (ACDL,out) and solid digestate (ACDs,out) via a sedimentation tank. Similar to Scenario 3, the solid digestate is directly used to produce soil fertilizer by the composting process and liquid digestate flows into the HRAP to produce algae. The microalgae biomass is harvested through the settler and centrifugation units. The harvested algae biomass is split into two recycled streams. One of the streams is mixed with pig feedstuff, and another one is fed into anaerobic co-digester. The energy demand for transportation from the anaerobic co-digester to the composting is required, and one emission from the composting is taken into consideration.

The algae-based wastewater treatment (AWWT) process shown in Scenarios 3 and 4 is composed of three equipment of HRAP, setter, and centrifugation, which is integrated with anaerobic mono-/co-digesters, respectively. This study assumes that the emissions from anaerobic mono-/co-digester, biogas plant, CHP unit, transportation, and AWWT process are ignored.

In the pig fattening process, the pig housing unit consumes electricity, heat, and feedstuff to produce pork after slaughtering and generates raw manure

which can be stored in an in-house storage unit. The traditional MMS is used to recover nitrogen and phosphorous compounds from manure for environmental compliance. By Fig. 1, the new MMS shows the process integration of anaerobic mono-/co-digester and algae-based wastewater treatment (AWWT) processes which can improve the biogas yield and provide the partial replacement of soybean meal by harvested algae biomass. Notably, the combined heat and power (CHP) unit is a renewable energy conversion option for generating heat and electricity by consuming biogas. In this study, four MMS scenarios integrated with the intensive pig farming system are denoted as Scenarios 1-4 shown in Fig. 2, are described as follows.

Scenario 1: The effluent of manure slurry from the in-house storage (MHSout) becomes soil fertilizer through the conventional outside stor- age and composting processes. Organic fertilizer can avoid or reduce synthetic fertilizer manufacture. The energy demand of transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and two emis- sions including OSemit as emissions of outside storage and CPemit as emissions of the composting are taken into consideration.

Scenario 2: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic mono-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue (AMDLS,out) The biogas flow (AMDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and elec- tricity through the CHP unit. The residue is a mixed liquid/solid diges- tate that can be used to produce soil fertilizer through the conventional outside storage and composting processes. The energy demand for the transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and two emissions from the outside storage and the composting are taken into consideration.

Scenario 3: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic mono-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue. The biogas flow (AMDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and electricity by the CHP unit. The residue is separated to liquid digestate (AMDL,out) and solid digestate (AMDs,out) via a sedimentation tank (Rathnasiri, 2016). The solid digestate is directly used to produce soil fertilizer by the composting process and liquid digestate is treated as the nutrient source for the growth of algae in the high rate algae pond (HRAP). The algae biomass is harvested through the settler and centrifugation units. The harvested algae is recycled to fully substitute partial nutrients and ad- ditives of swine diets. The energy demand of transportation from the outside storage to the composting is required, and one emission from the composting is taken into consideration.

Scenario 4: The manure flow from the in-house storage unit is pumped into anaerobic co-digester. This equipment can produce the biogas and remain the residue. The biogas flow (ACDv,out) is stored in the biogas plant and then be used to generate heat and electricity through the CHP unit. The residue is separated to liquid digestate (ACDL,out) and solid digestate (ACDs,out) via a sedimentation tank. Similar to Scenario 3, the solid digestate is directly used to produce soil fertilizer by the composting process and liquid digestate flows into the HRAP to produce algae. The microalgae biomass is harvested through the settler and centrifugation units. The harvested algae biomass is split into two recycled streams. One of the streams is mixed with pig feedstuff, and another one is fed into anaerobic co-digester. The energy demand for transportation from the anaerobic co-digester to the composting is required, and one emission from the composting is taken into consideration.

The algae-based wastewater treatment (AWWT) process shown in Scenarios 3 and 4 is composed of three equipment of HRAP, setter, and centrifugation, which is integrated with anaerobic mono-/co-digesters, respectively. This study assumes that the emissions from anaerobic mono-/co-digester, biogas plant, CHP unit, transportation, and AWWT process are ignored.

21: Scenarios 1 and 2 adopt the conventional manure management systems, and Scenarios 3 and 4, which are named as algae-based pig farming systems, consider the new MMS. These new MMS are based on a combination of anaerobic mono-/co-digester and AWWT, where the AWWT is denoted as a zero-emissions process if the water is fully recycled and all emissions of pollutants are zero. Moreover, LCA meth- odology is used to verify the merits of Scenarios 3 and 4 in terms of different levels of environmental impacts.
22: The algae-based pig farming system (Scenario 3 or 4) is attributable to the power and heat compensation, nutrient recovery, water recycling, and harvested algae production. The main background conditions for this pig farming system regarding materials, energy, and transport are described as follows.
(i) Pig feed is the main input. Soil fertilizer and emissions to air including the in-house (MHSemit), outside storage (OSemit), and composting (CPemit) are the main outputs.

30: The goal of this work is to estimate the environmental impacts of Scenarios 1-4 at midpoint and endpoint levels. According to the back- ground conditions and assumptions, the functional unit is defined as "1000 kg live weight of pig at the farm gate".

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) is aimed at understanding and evaluating the potential environmental impacts of the algae-based pig farming system. The ReCiPe (version 1.13) method which is avail- able as SimaPro® [About SimaPro] is used to evaluate the environ- mental impacts at midpoint and endpoint levels, where the midpoint level is quantified by 18 impact categories and the endpoint level is quantified by 3 impact categories.
The final phase of the LCA procedure interprets LCIA of four

scenarios at midpoint and endpoint levels. In this study, (i) the organic fertilizer by composting is used to fully substitute chemical fertilizers, (ii) the CHP for generating the heat and electricity is completely used for the pig farming systems, and (iii) the exhaust CO2 is completely injected into the algae pond. The life cycle environmental impact is usually validated by some comprehensive indicators such as life cycle carbon emission (i.e. COeq), life cycle energy demands, and eco-indicator point (Pt).

The nutrient recovery efficiency is often treated as an important index for the MMS-based pig farming system. Assumed that N loss only appears in the processes of in-house storage, outside storage, and com- posting and N loss for anaerobic mono-/co-digester and AWWT pro- cesses are ignored. In this study, the nutrient flow of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) is reduced to evaluate the nutrient recovery of N ratio

Assumed that the compositions of swine diets include cereals = 2460 kg, soybean meal - 30 kg, additives = 30 kg, and mineral (P) = 6 kg and the composition of the manure is not changed although harvested algae substitute additives and soybean meal. Moreover, the nutrient recovery of N ratio in Scenarios 1-4 are evaluated and depicted in Fig. 3(a), 3(b), 3(c), and 3(d), respectively. It is noted that the nutrient recovery of N ratio in Scenarios 3 and 4 are up to 81.4% and 78.5%, respectively. Compared to Scenario 1 with 58.0% N, it is validated that the anaerobic mono-/co-digester in place of the outside storage unit can effectively reduce N losses to air as well as increase the nutrient recovery efficiency. Nitrogen losses to air in Scenario 4 is higher than Scenario 3 because anaerobic co-digestion enhances the amount of solid digestate for composting. Besides, Scenario 4 with the substitution ratio for fertilizer by 34.3% is higher than Scenario 3 by 23%, but Scenario 4 with the substitution ratio for feed by 44.2% is lower than Scenario 3 by 58.4%.

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) is aimed at understanding and evaluating the potential environmental impacts of the algae-based pig farming system. The ReCiPe (version 1.13) method which is avail- able as SimaPro® [About SimaPro] is used to evaluate the environ- mental impacts at midpoint and endpoint levels, where the midpoint level is quantified by 18 impact categories and the endpoint level is quantified by 3 impact categories.
The final phase of the LCA procedure interprets LCIA of four

scenarios at midpoint and endpoint levels. In this study, (i) the organic fertilizer by composting is used to fully substitute chemical fertilizers, (ii) the CHP for generating the heat and electricity is completely used for the pig farming systems, and (iii) the exhaust CO2 is completely injected into the algae pond. The life cycle environmental impact is usually validated by some comprehensive indicators such as life cycle carbon emission (i.e. COeq), life cycle energy demands, and eco-indicator point (Pt).

The nutrient recovery efficiency is often treated as an important index for the MMS-based pig farming system. Assumed that N loss only appears in the processes of in-house storage, outside storage, and com- posting and N loss for anaerobic mono-/co-digester and AWWT pro- cesses are ignored. In this study, the nutrient flow of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) is reduced to evaluate the nutrient recovery of N ratio

Besides, Scenario 4 with the substitution ratio for fertilizer by 34.3% is higher than Scenario 3 by 23%, but Scenario 4 with the substitution ratio for feed by 44.2% is lower than Scenario 3 by 58.4%.

40: Fig. 4 shows the relative contribution of Scenarios 1-4 in terms of 18 impact categories. Scenario 1 with 100% relative contributions include fossil depletion (FD), natural land transformation (NLT), agricultural land occupation (ALO), freshwater ecotoxicity (FET), terrestrial eco- toxicity (TET), particulate matter formation (PMF), photochemical oxidation formation (POF), marine eutrophication (ME), freshwater eutrophication (FE), terrestrial acidification (TA), and climate change (CC). It implies that the conventional pig farming system (Scenario 1) has relatively high contributions on the increase of chemical pollutants such as NH3, freshwater consumption, energy consumption for com- posting, and agricultural land use. Scenario 2 with 100% relative con- tributions includes metal depletion (MD), ionizing radiation (IR), urban

land occupation (ULO), and human toxicity (HT). It implies that Sce- nario 2 has relatively high contributions to the decrease of impacts of MD, IR, ULO, and HT due to using the anaerobic mono-digester. Notably, the impact of metal depletion (MD) are all negative in four scenarios since the organic fertilizer by the composting can reduce the consumption of chemical fertilizer. The integration of anaerobic mono-/ co-digester and AWWT process as the new MMS used in Scenarios 3 and 4 ensures the lower environmental impacts due to alternative swine feedstuff, reuse treated water, restrict emissions of pollutants, and decrease field application.

42: The environmental impacts of Scenarios 1-4 at the endpoint level are depicted in Fig. 5(a) and (b), 5(c), and 5(d), respectively, where Pt is a unit to quantify three impacts categories. These figures show that the impact of the ecosystem in four scenarios is higher than other impacts due to the largest percentage of Pt values from the impact of feed pro- duction. The ecosystem impacts in Scenarios 3 and 4 are lower than Scenarios 1 and 2 due to the reduction of feed use. The impacts of HRAP slightly increase the total environmental impacts of Scenarios 3 and 4. Notably, the impacts of the manure utilization for energy production as like as the biogas plant and CHP are negative, SO it can slightly reduce the total environmental impacts of Scenarios 3 and 4. Moreover, Fig. 6 shows a comparison of the environmental impacts of Scenarios 1-4 at the endpoint level. It is noted that Scenarios 3 and 4 reduce the total environmental impacts by about 40% and 29%, respectively, compared to Scenario 1. Notably, two items of manure utilization for energy pro- duction and fertilizers have negative impact values, where the negative impact of manure utilization for energy production in Scenario 4 is larger than other Scenarios, but the item of feed production in four scenarios dominates the total impact values of the whole system. Table 5 shows that (i) Scenario 3 achieves the lowest total environmental impact (516 Pt) due to due to the highest nutrient recovery of N ratio (81%) and the replacement of soybean meal by using the fully recycled algae biomass, and (ii) Scenario 4 increases the algae biomass yield by adding the recycled algae biomass for the anaerobic co-digester SO that the biogas yield as well as heat and power from CHP.
43: The comparisons of four types of intensive pig farming systems concerning the substitution of swine feed, manure utilization for energy production, fertilizer, and algae biomass production are addressed. The significant conclusions of this work are summarized: (i) The algae-based pig farming systems (Scenarios 3 and 4) ensure the less gaseous nitrogen compound emissions than the conventional pig farming systems (Sce- narios 1 and 2); (ii) The algae-based pig farming systems ensure the lower environmental impacts than the conventional pig farming systems due to the AWWT process that increases the nutrient recovery of N ratio and replaces the outside storage process; (iii) From the viewpoint of environmental impacts, Scenario 3 is superior to Scenario 4 due to the harvested microalgae biomass that fully substitutes the partial in- gredients of swine diets; (iv) From the economic viewpoint, Scenario 4 is superior to Scenario 3 due to the anaerobic co-digester that increases the microalgae biomass yield as well as cogeneration from heat and power; (v) To improve the environmental impact of the AWWT process, the environmental impact assessment by SimaPro® shows that the energy- saving paddlewheel motor is a critical option to reduce the environ- mental impact of high rate algae pond (HRAP).
